title: The New Rules and CMO Configurations
text:
Core Argument
The new-rules section describes CMO configurations as propositions about what works for whom in what circumstances.

title: The New Rules and CMO Configurations
text:
Study Summary
The new-rules section describes CMO configurations as propositions about what works for whom in what circumstances.
A conjectured configuration is the starting point; a refined configuration is the evaluation finding.
The cumulative ambition is not exact replication of effects but better focusing of mechanisms across settings.

title: Qualitative Research & Evaluation Methods Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Match qualitative methods to questions about meaning, implementation, variation, and unanticipated effects.
Use observation and interviewing as disciplined ways to learn from context, not as informal anecdotes.
Document qualitative evidence systematically enough that interpretation can be challenged and refined.
Connect qualitative findings to decisions, not only to rich description.

title: Qualitative Research & Evaluation Methods Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as prompts during scoping, design review, method selection, and reporting. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s decision context, available evidence, and user needs.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Program Theory
Program theory is the explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to change. Across the book, it functions as a bridge between design, implementation, monitoring, evaluation, causal inference, and reporting. The recurring discipline is to make assumptions visible enough that they can be discussed, tested, revised, and used.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
section: Theory of Change and Theory of Action
text:
Theory of Change and Theory of Action
The book separates the theory of change from the theory of action. The theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem conditions to intended outcomes. The theory of action explains what the program does to activate that pathway: resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, implementation conditions, and assumptions about factors inside and outside the program’s control.
This distinction matters because a program may have a plausible desired change pathway but weak activities, or strong activities that do not address the causal factors identified in the situation analysis. The two theories need to be checked against each other.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Logic Models and Representation
Logic models are representations of program theory, not the theory itself. Pipeline models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and systems-informed diagrams each foreground different features. The right representation depends on intended use, intervention complexity, and audience.
A good representation explains contribution, makes links meaningful, avoids dead ends, shows direction of change, and removes detail that does not help interpretation.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Assumptions and Context
Assumptions appear throughout the book as testable claims about how change happens, what context permits, and what factors affect success. Program theory is strongest when assumptions are explicit enough to guide evidence collection and humble enough to be revised.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Causal Inference
Program theory supports causal inference by organizing congruence analysis, counterfactual comparison, and critical review. It does not replace causal evidence; it makes causal claims more inspectable by showing what patterns should be observed if the theory is operating.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting

Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 528-545 Chapter 16

This note summarises Chapter 16, “Synthesis and Reporting,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Core Argument
Synthesis and reporting should use program theory to integrate diverse evidence, explain causal contribution, support timely learning, and connect findings across linked evaluations.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Study Summary
The final chapter focuses on how evidence is brought together and reported. For a single evaluation, program theory can organize diverse evidence along the outcomes chain, helping readers see how implementation, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, context, and assumptions relate.
The authors also discuss rapid reporting and software-supported reporting. The key issue is not format but usefulness: reporting should make the evidence traceable to the theory and support decisions while the information is still actionable.
Across evaluations, program theory provides a structure for synthesis. It can connect findings from linked projects, compare evidence across different evaluations, and build cumulative understanding about what works, for whom, why, and under what conditions.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Key Concepts

Theory-based synthesis: integrating evidence according to the causal structure of the program theory.
Rapid reporting: timely communication designed to support ongoing decisions.
Cross-evaluation synthesis: using program theory to compare and integrate findings across studies.
Traceability: making clear how evidence supports conclusions and judgements.

Show where evidence is strong, weak, missing, or contradictory along the causal chain.
Use synthesis to refine theories for future programs and evaluations.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:

This note summarises Chapter 14, “Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Core Argument
Program theory can anchor monitoring and evaluation plans by identifying what to measure, when to measure it, what comparisons are needed, and how evaluation questions connect to the expected causal pathway.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shows how program theory becomes operational in performance monitoring and evaluation design. It helps decide which aspects of the situation, outcomes, success criteria, assumptions, implementation features, and causal links should be measured.
The authors stress choice. A theory may contain many possible indicators, but a monitoring system must prioritize measures that are feasible, useful, and tied to decisions. Evaluation planning then uses the theory to identify timing, purposes, key questions, information needs, methods, and comparisons.
The chapter also considers complicated and complex programs. In such cases, evaluation may need to examine variation across strands, sites, contexts, or emergent pathways rather than assume one uniform model. Program theory is useful when it guides these design choices rather than becoming a static appendix.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Key Concepts

Performance monitoring: routine tracking of selected aspects of implementation, context, outputs, outcomes, and assumptions.
Evaluation questions: focused questions that determine evidence needs and methods.
Comparison: a design feature used to interpret performance or causal contribution.
Theory-based measurement: choosing measures because they test or illuminate the program theory.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Practical Implications

Use the theory to decide what not to measure as well as what to measure.
Tie each indicator to a decision, assumption, or causal link.
Design evaluations that can distinguish implementation issues from weak causal theory.

This note summarises Chapter 12, “Some Common Program Archetypes,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Core Argument
Common program archetypes help evaluators recognize recurring intervention logics, but each archetype brings different assumptions, risks, and evidence needs.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Study Summary
The chapter organizes recurring program forms such as advisory, information, education, incentives, deterrence, case management, community capacity building, and direct service delivery. These archetypes are useful because similar intervention forms often rely on similar causal assumptions.
Each archetype points to different change mechanisms. Information programs assume attention, comprehension, relevance, and ability to act. Incentives assume behavior responds to rewards. Deterrence assumes detection, sanction, and perceived risk. Case management assumes coordinated support can address multiple barriers. Capacity building assumes communities or organizations can acquire and use new capability.
The authors’ point is not to classify programs for its own sake. Archetypes help evaluators ask sharper questions about whether the chosen strategy fits the problem, what conditions are required, and what evidence would show that the mechanism is operating.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Key Concepts

Program archetype: a recurring intervention pattern with characteristic causal assumptions.
Carrots and sticks: incentive and deterrence approaches to changing behavior.
Case management: coordinated support addressing multiple participant needs or barriers.
Capacity building: strengthening ability to act at individual, organizational, or community levels.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 320-345 Chapter 10

This note summarises Chapter 10, “Critiquing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be critiqued for clarity, plausibility, evidence, context, ethics, usefulness, and proportionality before it is used as the basis for monitoring or evaluation.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The chapter provides criteria for reviewing program theory. Internal criteria examine whether the description is clear, whether the outcomes chain organizes the theory, whether outcomes address the problem, whether mechanisms are articulated, and whether the logical argument is plausible.
External validation asks whether the theory is supported by research, consistent with accepted theories, attentive to context, ethically defensible, and appropriate for its intended use. This turns program theory into an evaluand: something that can itself be assessed and improved.
The authors also emphasize stakeholder engagement and response. A critique is valuable only if its results inform revision, evaluation design, or decisions about whether the theory is good enough for the intended purpose.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Internal critique: assessment of coherence, clarity, and logical structure.
External validation: assessment against evidence, accepted theory, context, ethics, and use.
Mechanism articulation: explaining how activities or conditions produce change.
Fitness for purpose: whether the theory is worth its cost for the intended use.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure

Clarity of Description

The Ethical Base for the Program Theory

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Review program theory before locking in indicators and methods.
Use critique to prioritize evidence gaps and assumptions to test.
Involve stakeholders in judging plausibility, context, and ethical acceptability.

text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 226-267 Chapter 8

This note summarises Chapter 8, “Developing a Theory of Action,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Core Argument
A theory of action explains what the program will do to activate the theory of change, including resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, assumptions, and factors that affect achievement.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Study Summary
Where Chapter 7 concentrates on the desired change pathway, Chapter 8 asks how the program is expected to make that pathway happen. The theory of action identifies resources, activities, outputs, implementation features, assumptions, and external factors that influence whether intended outcomes are achieved.
The chapter gives substantial attention to success criteria and desired attributes. These convert broad outcome language into evaluative standards, making it clearer what would count as good performance. It also emphasizes realistic success criteria, especially when outcomes depend on factors outside program control.
The authors connect theory of action and theory of change through comparison: activities and outputs should be checked against the factors believed to influence outcomes. If the program does not act on the causal factors identified in the theory of change, the model exposes a strategic mismatch.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of action: the account of what the program does and how delivery is expected to support change.
Success criteria: standards or desired attributes used to judge whether outcomes are good enough.
Program factors and nonprogram factors: influences inside and outside the intervention’s control.
Theory matrix: a way to align outcomes, assumptions, activities, and evidence needs.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Chapter Structure

Preparing The Theory Of Action

Assumptions About Program Factors
Assumptions About Nonprogram External Factors

Managing An Identified Nonprogram Factor: Mature Workers Program
How To Identify Factors That Are Likely To Affect Outcomes
Program-Specific Research and Investigation Techniques
Program Theory and General Research
Identifying What The Program Does

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Practical Implications

Define success before interpreting evidence.
Check whether activities are logically connected to the causal factors they are meant to influence.
Track external factors that can enable, constrain, or explain outcomes.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

This note summarises Chapter 6, “Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

This note summarises Chapter 6, “Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Developing a program theory is a methodological choice, not just a workshop activity; evaluators must decide who participates, what evidence informs the theory, how conflict is handled, how much resource to invest, and when to revisit the theory.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This long chapter sets out process options for identifying or developing program theory. It contrasts staff-led, evaluator-facilitated, stakeholder-engaged, deductive, inductive, and mental-model approaches, arguing that the right mix depends on purpose, context, evidence availability, and stakeholder politics.
The authors give detailed attention to workshops and interviews, but they treat these as tools within a broader design. Workshops can surface divergent theories, build ownership, and negotiate shared models; they can also suppress disagreement if poorly facilitated. Interviews and document review may be better when perspectives are sensitive or dispersed.
The chapter is also realistic about challenges: conflicting program theories, little research evidence, reluctance to disclose assumptions, weak engagement, and limited resources. The response is not to avoid theory development but to make trade-offs explicit and revisit the theory as evidence and circumstances change.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Mental models: implicit stakeholder beliefs about how the program works.
Deductive development: building from formal theory, prior research, or established causal knowledge.
Inductive development: building from observations, cases, data, and practice experience.
Relative program theory: a theory that is fit for a purpose and moment rather than final for all uses.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Process Options For Developing Program Theory
Decision 1: Who Should Be Involved In Developing A Program Theory, And How?
Development of Program Theories by Staff, Funders, and Others
Facilitation or Development by Program Theorists and Evaluators
Decisions About Other Stakeholders to Involve
Decision 2: What Is An Appropriate Mix Of Approaches For Developing Or Eliciting The Program Theory?
Articulating Stakeholder Mental Models
Deductive Development
Inductive Development
Choosing an Approach: Articulating Mental Models, Deductive Approaches, or Inductive Approaches?
An Iterative Process Combining All Three Approaches To Program Theory Building
Implications of Choice of Approach for Who Should Be Involved
Decision 3: How Might Workshops And Interviews Be Used In Developing Program Theory?
Workshop Processes

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Design the theory-development process as carefully as the representation.
Combine stakeholder knowledge, research evidence, and empirical observation where possible.
Document disagreements and uncertainty instead of forcing premature consensus.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 82-95 Chapter 4

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 82-95 Chapter 4

This note summarises Chapter 4, “Scoping Intended Uses,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be scoped around intended users, uses, and timing; the same intervention can need different theories for planning, communication, management, monitoring, evaluation, or evidence-informed policy.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shifts attention from what a program theory contains to what it is for. A theory intended for early design and stakeholder alignment may need different detail from one used for performance monitoring, impact evaluation, or synthesis across programs.
The authors emphasize users as well as uses. Funders, managers, delivery staff, partners, participants, evaluators, and policymakers may need different representations and may also bring different knowledge about how the intervention is expected to work. Timing matters because a theory built before implementation supports design choices, while a theory reconstructed later supports evaluation and learning from what actually happened.
The chapter presents program theory as a flexible tool across the program cycle. Its usefulness depends on making the intended use explicit before deciding who participates, what evidence is needed, how detailed the model should be, and how formal the representation needs to become.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Key Concepts

Intended users: people who will directly use the program theory or evaluation findings.
Intended uses: the decisions, actions, or learning processes the theory should support.
Timing: the point in the program cycle when the theory is developed or revised.
Use-sensitive design: tailoring theory development and representation to practical use.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

Why Intended Use Matters
Considering Intended Users
Considering Timing
Considering Intended and Other Possible Uses
Using Program Theory
Using Program Theory for Planning
Using Program Theory for Managing, Engaging Stakeholders, and Communicating
Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation
Using Program Theory for Evidence-Based Policy and Practice
Summary
Exercises

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Practical Implications

Begin each program theory task with a use case, not a template.
Identify who must understand, own, challenge, or apply the theory.
Revise the theory when the program moves from design to implementation to evaluation.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

This note summarises Chapter 2, “Variations of Program Theory over Time,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Core Argument
Program theory is not a new invention but a family of related ideas with varied terminology, intellectual roots, and practical traditions; evaluators need to understand the vocabulary well enough to choose terms that support use rather than confusion.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Study Summary
The chapter places program theory in a longer history of evaluation, planning, social science, and management practice. Rather than presenting one settled method, it shows how different traditions have emphasized causal explanation, planning logic, accountability, learning, stakeholder communication, and evidence synthesis.
A substantial part of the chapter is terminological. Terms such as program theory, theory of change, theory of action, logic model, logframe, results chain, outcomes chain, and evaluation model are used differently across organizations and countries. The authors treat terminology as consequential because vocabulary shapes what people attend to and what they overlook.
The chapter distinguishes key ideas without insisting on one universal label. The practical message is to define terms explicitly in each project, align language with users, and avoid assuming that a familiar diagram or phrase carries the same meaning for every stakeholder.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of change: the causal account of how and why change is expected to happen.
Theory of action: the account of what the program will do to activate or support the theory of change.
Logic model: a representation of program components and expected links, often but not always diagrammatic.
Terminological fit: choosing words that make the theory usable for the intended audience.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Chapter Structure
The extracted chapter headings emphasize these topics:

A Short History Of Program Theory
Philosophical Roots
Early Examples
The First Boom Period
Innovations
The Current State
Terminology In Program Theory
Advantages and Disadvantages of Different Terminology
Terms for Components in a Pipeline Model
Australia: AusAID (Australian Agency for International Development) (Logframe)
Canada: Treasury
India: Comptroller and Auditor-General
South Africa: Framework for Managing Programme Performance Information, National Treasury
United States: University of Wisconsin Extension Service

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Practical Implications

Define the working vocabulary at the start of a program theory exercise.
Expect organizational templates to encode assumptions about what counts as a good theory.
Treat debates over terms as design signals, not merely semantic disputes.

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Core Argument
The section warns that logic models can become misleading when programs combine intervention delivery with wider system change.

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Study Summary
The section warns that logic models can become misleading when programs combine intervention delivery with wider system change.
The problem is not that logic models are useless, but that they need to be applied with judgment about what the program is trying to change and how stable the causal path is.
When the model cannot represent system dynamics or multiple layers of action, evaluators should supplement it with richer program theory.

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Key Concepts

System change
Model fit
Program complexity
Representation limits

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Practical Implications

Check whether the model represents both the intervention and the system it seeks to change.
Look for confusion caused by compressing multiple causal routes into one linear chain.
Revise the representation before it drives data collection.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Core Argument
The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Study Summary
The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.
A useful design does more than select methods. It asks whether the program is described coherently enough to support evaluation and whether stakeholders need a logic model, a fuller program theory, or both.
The practical lesson is proportionality: choose enough structure to make the evaluation credible and useful without forcing every program into the same template.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Key Concepts

Evaluation purpose
Program description
Method fit
Stakeholder agreement

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Practical Implications

Do not move to methods before the program description is adequate.
Use design conversations to surface what stakeholders expect the evaluation to decide.
Escalate from a logic model to program theory when context or causal mechanisms need to be examined.

Core Argument
Practical evaluation design should match program stage, stakeholder needs, program theory, and intended use, choosing among constructive, conclusive, and hybrid approaches rather than defaulting to one method or one model of evidence.

title: Practical Program Evaluation Chapter Map
text:
How to Use This Map

Start with Chapters 1-3 to select the evaluation route and clarify program theory.
Use Chapters 4-5 when the program is still being planned and needs program-scope or action-plan work.
Use Chapters 6-8 for early implementation, mature implementation, and monitoring-system questions.
Use Chapters 9-12 for outcome readiness, causal rigor, real-world effectuality, and theory-driven explanation.
Use Chapters 13-15 when logic models, theory sources, or dissemination assumptions need more advanced treatment.

title: Chapter 14: Formal Theories versus Stakeholder Theories in Interventions: Relative Strengths and Limitations
text:
Core Argument
Formal theory and stakeholder theory each strengthen and weaken different parts of program theory, so evaluators must clarify both change logic and action arrangements before judging an intervention.

title: Chapter 14: Formal Theories versus Stakeholder Theories in Interventions: Relative Strengths and Limitations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 14 compares interventions grounded mainly in formal theory with interventions grounded mainly in stakeholder theory.
Formal theory can provide a clearer literature-based change model, especially about determinants and causal assumptions. It may also come with protocols that help specify the intervention.
Stakeholder theory is often more locally grounded and practice-sensitive, but it is usually less explicit in documents and literature. Evaluators therefore need to help stakeholders articulate assumptions that may otherwise remain implicit.
The chapter’s practical lesson is diagnostic. Evaluators should ask which part of program theory is strong or weak: the change model, the action model, the intervention protocol, implementation arrangements, or the fit between local practice and formal theory.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses formal theory, stakeholder theory, program theory, and change model while moving through formal theory-based interventions, stakeholder theory-based interventions, and change model clarification. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the advanced sequence: it tests the limits of common tools and connects evaluation evidence to theory, system change, and dissemination.

title: Chapter 14: Formal Theories versus Stakeholder Theories in Interventions: Relative Strengths and Limitations
text:
Key Concepts

formal theory: A theory from research literature or a scientific tradition used to design or explain an intervention.
stakeholder theory: Program theory based primarily on stakeholders’ experience, assumptions, and local knowledge.
program theory: The explicit or implicit assumptions linking action, context, mechanisms, and outcomes in an intervention.
change model: The program theory component explaining how intervention activities are expected to change determinants and outcomes.
action model: The program theory component specifying the organizations, implementers, partners, context, target population, and delivery arrangements needed for action.
intervention protocol: The specification of intervention content, sequence, dosage, materials, activities, and delivery expectations.
determinants: The conditions, behaviors, beliefs, capacities, or mechanisms an intervention must change to reach desired outcomes.

title: Chapter 14: Formal Theories versus Stakeholder Theories in Interventions: Relative Strengths and Limitations
text:
Chapter Structure

Formal theory-based interventions.
Stakeholder theory-based interventions.
Change model clarification.
Action model clarification.
Relative strengths.
Relative limitations.

title: Chapter 14: Formal Theories versus Stakeholder Theories in Interventions: Relative Strengths and Limitations
text:
Practical Implications

Use formal theory where it clarifies determinants and mechanisms.
Do not assume local stakeholder theory is explicit enough for evaluation.
Assess change and action models separately when comparing intervention theories.

title: Chapter 12: The Theory-driven Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Theory-driven outcome evaluation explains how, why, and under what implementation conditions outcomes occur by examining mechanisms as well as effects.

title: Chapter 12: The Theory-driven Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 12 upgrades outcome evaluation from a works-or-does-not-work question to an explanatory assessment of transformation processes.
The theory-driven approach examines program theory, causal mechanisms, and implementation. It is placed under the enlightenment strategy because it helps stakeholders understand why a program succeeds, fails, or works only under certain conditions.
Chen distinguishes intervening mechanism evaluation, moderating mechanism evaluation, and integrative process/outcome evaluation. These designs focus respectively on mediating processes, conditioning factors, and the full connection between implementation and outcomes.
The integrative process/outcome approach is the most comprehensive. It checks implementation assumptions and causal processes together, showing that implementation success is necessary but not sufficient for program success.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses theory-driven outcome evaluation, intervening mechanism, moderating mechanism, and integrative process/outcome evaluation while moving through rationale for theory-driven outcome evaluation, clarifying stakeholder theory, and types of mechanism evaluation. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the outcome sequence: it moves from readiness for outcome evaluation to experimentation, real-world effectuality, and theory-driven explanation.

title: Chapter 12: The Theory-driven Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

theory-driven outcome evaluation: Outcome evaluation that examines causal mechanisms and implementation processes, not only whether outcomes changed.
intervening mechanism: A mediator-like causal process through which an intervention affects determinants or outcomes.
moderating mechanism: A conditioning process or factor that changes whether, how, or for whom an intervention affects outcomes.
integrative process/outcome evaluation: Theory-driven evaluation that jointly examines implementation assumptions, causal processes, and outcomes.
implementation success: A state in which action model delivery activates the intended change process, while still requiring evidence that the change model works.
causal mechanism: The process through which an intervention is expected to affect determinants or outcomes.
program theory: The explicit or implicit assumptions linking action, context, mechanisms, and outcomes in an intervention.

title: Chapter 12: The Theory-driven Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Chapter Structure

Rationale for theory-driven outcome evaluation.
Clarifying stakeholder theory.
Types of mechanism evaluation.
Moderating mechanisms.
Integrative process/outcome evaluation.
Mixed methods.

title: Chapter 12: The Theory-driven Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Use theory-driven outcome evaluation when stakeholders need explanation, not only verdicts.
Match design to whether the question concerns mediation, moderation, or whole-program operation.
Interpret implementation success as necessary but not sufficient for outcome success.

title: Chapter 10: The Experimentation Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The experimentation approach prioritizes internal validity and pure independent effects, making it valuable for some conclusive outcome questions but incomplete for many real-world evaluation needs.

title: Chapter 10: The Experimentation Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 10 presents the dominant conclusive outcome tradition: validity-focused outcome evaluation guided by experimentation logic.
The approach asks whether an intervention caused intended outcomes by ruling out threats to internal validity. It therefore values research designs that support credible causal inference, especially experimental and strong quasi-experimental designs.
Chen treats the approach seriously but conditionally. It is appropriate when stakeholders need rigorous evidence about whether an intervention affected outcomes apart from confounding factors.
The chapter also prepares the critique developed in Chapter 11. A design that maximizes internal validity may bracket the real-world supports and adaptations that stakeholders actually use to make interventions work.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses validity-focused outcome evaluation, experimentation evaluation approach, internal validity, and pure independent effects while moving through experimentation approach, Campbellian validity logic, and threats to internal validity. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the outcome sequence: it moves from readiness for outcome evaluation to experimentation, real-world effectuality, and theory-driven explanation.

title: Chapter 10: The Experimentation Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

validity-focused outcome evaluation: Outcome evaluation guided by experimentation logic and the priority of ruling out threats to internal validity.
experimentation evaluation approach: Outcome evaluation that prioritizes experimental or quasi-experimental logic to estimate pure causal effects.
internal validity: The credibility of a causal claim that observed outcomes were produced by the intervention rather than confounding influences.
pure independent effects: Effects attributed to an intervention after removing or controlling for other influences and supports.
randomized experiment: A causal design that uses random assignment to strengthen claims about intervention effects.
threats to validity: Alternative explanations, biases, or design problems that can weaken causal or evaluative claims.
conclusive outcome evaluation: Outcome evaluation used to judge whether an intervention achieved intended effects or merit.

title: Chapter 10: The Experimentation Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Chapter Structure

Experimentation approach.
Campbellian validity logic.
Threats to internal validity.
Research designs.
Strengths.
Limits.

title: Chapter 10: The Experimentation Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Use experimentation logic when pure causal attribution is the central question.
Make validity threats explicit in outcome design.
Do not assume internal-validity priority answers every stakeholder question.

title: Chapter 08: Program Monitoring and the Development of a Monitoring System
text:
Core Argument
Program monitoring supplies periodic process and outcome indicators for management and accountability, but it complements rather than replaces deeper process or outcome evaluation.

title: Chapter 08: Program Monitoring and the Development of a Monitoring System
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 8 explains program monitoring as periodic quantitative collection of process and outcome information. It provides vital statistics about implementation and client progress.
Chen distinguishes monitoring from evaluation. Monitoring indicators can show direction, volume, coverage, and progress, but they usually do not provide the depth needed to explain implementation quality or establish outcome effectiveness.
Process monitoring tracks service delivery and implementation. Outcome monitoring tracks client or program outcomes over time. The chapter argues that organizations often need an integrated monitoring system that connects both.
Monitoring systems require organizational capacity, data routines, information systems, and accountability uses. Their value depends on whether indicators are meaningful and whether stakeholders understand the limits of what monitoring can claim.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses program monitoring, process monitoring, outcome monitoring, and performance monitoring while moving through definition of monitoring, process monitoring, and outcome monitoring. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the implementation sequence: it separates early troubleshooting, mature implementation assessment, and routine monitoring.

title: Chapter 08: Program Monitoring and the Development of a Monitoring System
text:
Key Concepts

program monitoring: Periodic quantitative tracking of program process and outcomes to support management and accountability.
process monitoring: Repeated tracking of implementation and service-delivery indicators.
outcome monitoring: Periodic tracking of client or program outcomes that informs progress but does not by itself establish effectiveness.
performance monitoring: A merit-assessment strategy that repeatedly tracks indicators of process or outcome performance.
indicator: A measurable sign of process or outcome performance used in monitoring or evaluation.
monitoring system: The organizational data routines, indicators, tools, and information infrastructure used for ongoing program monitoring.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support responsibility, reporting, judgment, and program oversight.

title: Chapter 08: Program Monitoring and the Development of a Monitoring System
text:
Chapter Structure

Definition of monitoring.
Process monitoring.
Outcome monitoring.
Monitoring versus evaluation.
Organizational systems.
Using monitoring data.

title: Chapter 08: Program Monitoring and the Development of a Monitoring System
text:
Practical Implications

Build monitoring indicators around decisions and accountability needs.
Avoid treating monitoring trends as causal impact evidence.
Integrate process and outcome monitoring when both implementation and progress matter.

title: Chapter 06: Constructive Process Evaluation Tailored for the Initial Implementation
text:
Core Argument
Initial implementation requires constructive process evaluation that rapidly identifies delivery problems, supports troubleshooting, and helps stakeholders stabilize the program.

title: Chapter 06: Constructive Process Evaluation Tailored for the Initial Implementation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 6 moves from planning to early implementation. At this stage, rules, procedures, staffing, recruitment, and delivery routines are still fluid, so the main evaluation need is timely information for improvement.
Formative evaluation is the main troubleshooting approach. It uses flexible methods to identify barriers and facilitators, distinguish implementation problems from design problems, and help stakeholders make corrections while the program is still taking shape.
Program review/development meetings are another troubleshooting tool, especially for surfacing staff experience and building shared understanding of implementation problems. Chen is clear that these meetings are mainly internal improvement tools, not substitutes for accountability evaluation.
Bilateral empowerment evaluation extends the evaluator role into partnership. It can maximize the use of evaluation information during development, but it also creates limits for later independent accountability assessment.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses initial implementation, constructive evaluation, formative evaluation, and troubleshooting while moving through initial implementation concerns, formative evaluation, and program review/development meetings. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the implementation sequence: it separates early troubleshooting, mature implementation assessment, and routine monitoring.

title: Chapter 06: Constructive Process Evaluation Tailored for the Initial Implementation
text:
Key Concepts

initial implementation: The early delivery stage when routines are still being established and constructive troubleshooting is often needed.
constructive evaluation: Evaluation used to improve, clarify, develop, or strengthen a program before or during implementation.
formative evaluation: Development-oriented evaluation during implementation that identifies barriers, facilitators, and remedies.
troubleshooting: A strategy for quickly identifying implementation problems and supporting stakeholders in fixing them.
program review/development meeting: A facilitated internal meeting used to identify implementation difficulties and possible remedies.
bilateral empowerment evaluation: A partnership form in which evaluators and stakeholders jointly use evaluation information while developing and stabilizing a program.
implementation problem: A barrier, failure, inconsistency, or unexpected condition that interferes with delivering the program as intended.

title: Chapter 06: Constructive Process Evaluation Tailored for the Initial Implementation
text:
Chapter Structure

Initial implementation concerns.
Formative evaluation.
Program review/development meetings.
Bilateral empowerment evaluation.
Pros and cons.
Selection of approaches.

title: Chapter 06: Constructive Process Evaluation Tailored for the Initial Implementation
text:
Practical Implications

Use fast feedback when implementation routines are unstable.
Do not confuse internal troubleshooting meetings with accountability evidence.
Plan for role changes if evaluators later need to provide independent assessment.

title: Chapter 04: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Program Scope
text:
Core Argument
Program-scope work clarifies the problem, goals, target population, intervention, determinants, and expected outcomes before stakeholders commit to an action plan.

title: Chapter 04: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Program Scope
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 4 begins the planning-stage sequence by distinguishing program scope from action plan. Program scope explains what problem the program addresses, why it matters, who is targeted, what intervention is proposed, and how the intervention should produce outcomes.
Needs assessment and formative research provide background information for program scope. Needs assessment helps identify unmet needs and target priorities; formative research deepens understanding of clients, implementers, culture, and setting.
The chapter uses the change model to help evaluators and stakeholders specify goals, determinants, interventions, and target populations. This makes the program’s rationale explicit enough to be tested and improved.
Relevancy testing is presented as a troubleshooting approach. It checks whether goals are realistic, whether target groups fit the problem, and whether the proposed intervention is relevant to the determinants and needs identified.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses program scope, change model, needs assessment, and formative research while moving through program plan, program scope, and background information provision. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the planning sequence: it helps stakeholders clarify program scope and action plan before implementation begins.

title: Chapter 04: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Program Scope
text:
Key Concepts

program scope: The planning account of problem, goals, target population, intervention, determinants, and expected outcomes.
change model: The program theory component explaining how intervention activities are expected to change determinants and outcomes.
needs assessment: Planning-stage inquiry into unmet needs, service gaps, target groups, and priorities.
formative research: Background inquiry used during planning to understand needs, culture, setting, and stakeholder or client perspectives.
target population: The people, groups, organizations, or communities the program intends to reach or affect.
relevancy testing: A troubleshooting approach for checking whether program scope assumptions fit the problem, target group, and proposed intervention.
determinants: The conditions, behaviors, beliefs, capacities, or mechanisms an intervention must change to reach desired outcomes.

title: Chapter 04: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Program Scope
text:
Chapter Structure

Program plan.
Program scope.
Background information provision.
Needs assessment.
Formative research.
Relevancy testing.

title: Chapter 04: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Program Scope
text:
Practical Implications

Do not let action planning proceed without a coherent program scope.
Use needs and formative evidence to correct weak assumptions early.
Test whether goals, target groups, determinants, and interventions fit one another.

title: Chapter 02: Understand Approaches to Evaluation and Select Ones That Work: The Comprehensive Evaluation Typology
text:
Core Argument
The comprehensive evaluation typology matches program stage, stakeholder purpose, evaluation approach, and strategy so evaluators can choose approaches that fit the situation.

title: Chapter 02: Understand Approaches to Evaluation and Select Ones That Work: The Comprehensive Evaluation Typology
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 2 expands the Chapter 1 typology into a life-cycle map. It organizes evaluation around four program stages: planning, initial implementation, mature implementation, and outcome.
The comprehensive typology is the book’s main routing device. It associates each stage with purposes, approaches, and strategies such as background information provision, development facilitation, troubleshooting, merit assessment, performance monitoring, performance assessment, enlightenment, and partnership.
The chapter stresses that evaluation is situational. An approach that is useful for a mature program can be premature or misleading for an immature program, and a method that supports accountability may not help stakeholders fix urgent implementation problems.
Chen also distinguishes single-entry and multiple-entry evaluation. Readers can enter the book at the chapter that matches the current program stage, or combine chapters when an evaluation spans planning, implementation, monitoring, and outcomes.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses comprehensive evaluation typology, program stage, evaluation strategy, and single-entry evaluation while moving through program life cycle, evaluation purposes, and approaches by stage. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the foundations sequence: it defines evaluation fit, typology, and program theory before any stage-specific evaluation is selected.

title: Chapter 02: Understand Approaches to Evaluation and Select Ones That Work: The Comprehensive Evaluation Typology
text:
Key Concepts

comprehensive evaluation typology: Chen’s map of evaluation approaches by program stage, evaluation function, and strategy.
program stage: A phase in the program life cycle, such as planning, initial implementation, mature implementation, or outcome.
evaluation strategy: The broad purpose and orientation that links program stage, stakeholder need, evaluation approach, and use.
single-entry evaluation: An evaluation design focused on one program stage.
multiple-entry evaluation: An evaluation design that addresses two or more program stages rather than one isolated stage.
performance assessment: A merit-assessment strategy that uses more rigorous evidence to judge implementation or outcome performance.
development facilitation: A strategy that uses evaluation tools to help stakeholders design, clarify, or improve a program.

title: Chapter 02: Understand Approaches to Evaluation and Select Ones That Work: The Comprehensive Evaluation Typology
text:
Chapter Structure

Program life cycle.
Evaluation purposes.
Approaches by stage.
Strategies underlying approaches.
Applying the typology.
Single-entry and multiple-entry routes.

title: Chapter 02: Understand Approaches to Evaluation and Select Ones That Work: The Comprehensive Evaluation Typology
text:
Practical Implications

Use program stage as the first routing question.
Select approaches by stakeholder need, not by evaluator preference.
Use multiple-entry designs when a real assignment crosses stages.

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the expanded notes from Magenta Book: Central Government guidance on evaluation (HM Treasury and Evaluation Task Force 2020).

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the expanded notes from Magenta Book: Central Government guidance on evaluation (HM Treasury and Evaluation Task Force 2020).

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Plan evaluation with the intervention, business case, monitoring, and benefits management arrangements.
Start scoping with users, decisions, theory of change, assumptions, and priority evaluation questions.
Choose methods by question, causal claim, data feasibility, ethics, burden, and use rather than by familiarity.
Use mixed methods when process, impact, value for money, explanation, and implementation learning all matter.
Resolve data access, linkage, data quality, and protection issues before the evaluation depends on them.
Create governance that can quality assure design, tools, analysis, interpretation, and reporting while still adapting to change.
Plan dissemination, publication, and transparency at the start so findings can be used and trusted.
Use appendix guidance when specialist risks arise: complexity, realist explanation, AI interventions, qualitative quality, transparency, PIRs, or test-and-learn work.
Review capability before commissioning and build teams around the skills the evaluation actually requires.

title: Magenta Book Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as checks during scoping, proposal decisions, design review, method selection, data planning, governance, reporting, publication, and capability development. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s context, stakes, resources, data constraints, stakeholder needs, and transparency obligations.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Core Argument
The executive summary positions evaluation as a government learning and accountability discipline that must combine evaluation types, methods, stages, and standards.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Study Summary
The summary frames evaluation as a way to understand whether interventions are designed well, delivered as intended, producing results, and offering value for money. It resists treating process, impact, and economic evaluation as isolated exercises.
Learning and accountability are the main reasons for evaluation. Learning helps government manage uncertainty, improve live interventions, and build evidence for future policy. Accountability helps departments explain outcomes, spending, and public value to decision-makers, scrutiny bodies, and the public.
The summary also acts as a route map. It identifies the core chapters on scoping, methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability, then points to annexes that deepen specialist topics such as analytical methods, quality, realist evaluation, complexity, transparency, AI, and test-and-learn practice.
For close reading, track how this note uses learning, accountability, process evaluation, and impact evaluation while moving through learning and accountability purposes, key evaluation terminology, and core chapter route. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, the executive summary is the orientation point: it explains why process, impact, and value for money evidence need to be planned together across the guidance.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Key Concepts

learning: Use of evaluation to reduce uncertainty, improve interventions, and strengthen future policy design.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.
process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
evaluation design: The plan that connects evaluation purpose, questions, theory, data, methods, governance, analysis, and use.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Executive Structure

Learning and accountability purposes.
Key evaluation terminology.
Core chapter route.
Annex and supplementary guidance route.

title: Executive Summary and Evaluation Types
text:
Practical Implications

Treat evaluation type as a design decision linked to the decision problem, not a label added after commissioning.
Plan process, impact, and value for money evidence together when the intervention needs an integrated judgment.
Use the chapter and annex route to find the right level of technical detail for the evaluation decision at hand.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation findings have value only if their users, decision points, products, publication route, and transparency commitments are planned from the start.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 6 treats use and dissemination as design issues, not communication tasks left until the report is drafted. Evaluators need to know which groups need what information, when they need it, and what form will make it usable.
The chapter distinguishes direct and indirect users. Direct users may need findings for live policy decisions, spending reviews, or delivery improvement; indirect users may need learning for future policy, scrutiny, or wider public understanding.
The dissemination plan should be agreed early with stakeholders, communication teams, and ministerial offices where relevant. This reduces surprises and protects publication decisions from appearing to depend on whether findings are favourable.
Openness and transparency are central. Registration, published protocols, clear reporting, accessible limitations, and publication expectations strengthen trust and public value.
For close reading, track how this note uses evaluation use, dissemination, transparency, and evaluation registry while moving through users and evidence needs, use and dissemination plan, and tailored reporting. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Key Concepts

evaluation use: The application of evaluation evidence in policy decisions, delivery improvement, accountability, or learning.
dissemination: Planned communication of findings to audiences who need evidence for decisions, scrutiny, or learning.
transparency: Making evaluation plans, evidence, analysis, and findings open enough to support trust, challenge, and reuse.
evaluation registry: A transparency mechanism for recording planned, live, and completed government evaluations.
reporting transparency: Clear reporting of methods, uncertainty, robustness, limitations, and interpretation.
pre-registration: Documenting evaluation plans or analysis choices before data collection or analysis to reduce selective reporting.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Chapter Structure

Users and evidence needs.
Use and dissemination plan.
Tailored reporting.
Publication.
Openness and transparency.

title: Chapter 6: The Use and Dissemination of Evaluation Findings
text:
Practical Implications

Start dissemination planning when the evaluation is scoped, not when findings arrive.
Tailor outputs to audience decisions while keeping limitations and uncertainty visible.
Agree publication and transparency expectations early enough to protect credibility.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation data must be planned with the intervention, because evidence quality, access, linkage, and governance determine what questions can be answered.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 4 treats data planning as central to evaluation design. Data access, collection, and linkage need attention while the intervention is still being designed; otherwise the eventual evaluation may be weak, expensive, or impossible.
The chapter distinguishes existing administrative and monitoring data, long-term surveys, and new primary data collection. Existing data can reduce cost and burden, but it may not align exactly with evaluation questions or collect measures at the right time.
Triangulation strengthens confidence by combining sources such as monitoring data, surveys, interviews, social media data, and bespoke research. Data quality checks, piloting, bias reduction, and clear definitions matter across all sources.
The chapter also foregrounds data protection, access protocols, and data linking. Linking can enrich evaluation datasets, but it raises governance, security, legal, ethical, and practical issues that must be resolved early.
For close reading, track how this note uses data quality, data access, administrative data, and monitoring while moving through data requirements, existing and new data sources, and administrative and monitoring data. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Key Concepts

data quality: The fitness of evaluation data, including relevance, validity, completeness, timeliness, and bias control.
data access: The legal, ethical, technical, and practical ability to obtain data needed for evaluation questions.
administrative data: Operational data collected by public services or delivery systems that can support evaluation if fit for purpose.
monitoring: Routine tracking of delivery, outputs, performance, or benefits that can inform but does not replace evaluation.
triangulation: Using multiple evidence sources or methods to test, compare, and strengthen interpretation.
data linking: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Chapter Structure

Data requirements.
Existing and new data sources.
Administrative and monitoring data.
Data quality.
Data protection.
Linkage.

title: Chapter 4: Data Collection, Data Access and Data Linking
text:
Practical Implications

Specify data needs during intervention design, not after delivery is complete.
Check whether existing administrative data measure the evaluation concepts directly enough.
Plan data sharing, linkage, ethics, and quality assurance before fieldwork or analysis starts.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Core Argument
Scoping turns an intervention, its theory of change, users, and uncertainties into answerable evaluation questions and a feasible design route.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 2 treats scoping as the disciplined bridge between policy need and evaluation design. Evaluators need to understand the intervention, its context, its theory of change, and what decisions the evaluation must inform.
A theory of change is used to identify assumptions, mechanisms, outcomes, data needs, and evidence gaps. The chapter stresses that evaluation approaches can be complementary: process evidence can support theory-based impact claims, and value for money work usually depends on impact evidence.
Evaluation questions are a control point. A long list of possible questions has to be prioritised into a smaller set of high-level questions that can guide design and remain manageable across delivery.
Scoping also includes practical feasibility: data access, timing, resources, stakeholder needs, ethical issues, transparency, and whether planned methods can answer the priority questions.
For close reading, track how this note uses scoping, theory of change, evaluation question, and evaluation design while moving through understanding the intervention, theory of change, and evaluation types and approaches. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this chapter is part of the core sequence from rationale and scoping through methods, data, management, dissemination, and capability.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Key Concepts

scoping: Early work to clarify the intervention, theory of change, users, questions, data, methods, and feasibility.
theory of change: An explanation of how an intervention is expected to produce outcomes, including assumptions and mechanisms.
evaluation question: A focused question that determines what evidence and analysis an evaluation must provide.
evaluation design: The plan that connects evaluation purpose, questions, theory, data, methods, governance, analysis, and use.
data access: The legal, ethical, technical, and practical ability to obtain data needed for evaluation questions.
pre-registration: Documenting evaluation plans or analysis choices before data collection or analysis to reduce selective reporting.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Chapter Structure

Understanding the intervention.
Theory of change.
Evaluation types and approaches.
Evaluation questions.
Design and feasibility.
Registration.

title: Chapter 2: Evaluation Scoping
text:
Practical Implications

Use the theory of change to decide what evidence matters, not only to describe the programme.
Prioritise evaluation questions before committing to methods or supplier specifications.
Document design changes transparently when feasibility or evidence changes during implementation.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Core Argument
The TIGER annex operationalises open science for government evaluation through proportionate planning, analysis, and reporting transparency.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Study Summary
The transparency annex adapts open science practices to government evaluation. It aims to make evaluation plans, analysis, and reporting transparent enough to strengthen integrity, trust, reuse, and value for money.
The annex is explicitly proportionate. Higher stakes, higher cost, high-profile, high learning value, novel, or complex evaluations should receive stronger transparency practices, while legal and data constraints still matter.
Planning transparency includes registration, evaluation plans, protocols, statistical analysis plans, timestamping, and archiving. These practices create a record before data collection or analysis can be shaped by results.
Analysis and reporting transparency require preserving materials, data, code, and methodological choices; communicating uncertainty; and showing whether findings are robust to reasonable alternative choices.
For close reading, track how this note uses transparency, open science, evaluation registry, and pre-registration while moving through transparency rationale, proportionality, and planning transparency. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Key Concepts

transparency: Making evaluation plans, evidence, analysis, and findings open enough to support trust, challenge, and reuse.
open science: Practices that make research plans, materials, analysis, and outputs more transparent and accessible.
evaluation registry: A transparency mechanism for recording planned, live, and completed government evaluations.
pre-registration: Documenting evaluation plans or analysis choices before data collection or analysis to reduce selective reporting.
analysis transparency: Preserving and, where proportionate, sharing the materials, data, code, and choices behind evaluation analysis.
reporting transparency: Clear reporting of methods, uncertainty, robustness, limitations, and interpretation.
robustness: The extent to which findings remain credible under reasonable checks, assumptions, and alternative analyses.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Appendix Structure

Transparency rationale.
Proportionality.
Planning transparency.
Analysis transparency.
Reporting transparency.
Tools and recommendations.

title: Transparency in Government Evaluation Research
text:
Practical Implications

Decide transparency practices during design, before data collection or analysis begins.
Archive plans, materials, code, and analysis decisions even when public release is not possible.
Report uncertainty and robustness in language decision-makers can understand.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Core Argument
The PIR guide adapts Magenta Book evaluation logic to regulatory review, where proportional evidence, monitoring data, stakeholder evidence, and feasible counterfactuals are central.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Study Summary
The regulatory post implementation review guide focuses on evaluating whether regulation remains justified, effective, proportionate, and fit for purpose after implementation.
Preparation involves clarifying the measure, baseline, intended effects, monitoring arrangements, existing evidence, proportionality, and review obligations. Where early planning was missed, the guide still pushes reviewers to build the best feasible evidence base.
PIR methods often combine monitoring data, stakeholder engagement, process evidence, impact evidence, and value for money considerations. Strong counterfactual impact designs may be difficult when regulation applies to all affected parties, so limitations need to be explicit.
Case material is used to show how different review strategies can meet scrutiny needs when evidence, timing, or implementation constraints vary.
For close reading, track how this note uses monitoring, counterfactual, impact evaluation, and process evaluation while moving through glossary and scope, preparing for a PIR, and monitoring data. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Key Concepts

monitoring: Routine tracking of delivery, outputs, performance, or benefits that can inform but does not replace evaluation.
counterfactual: An estimate of what would have happened without the intervention, used to support impact claims.
impact evaluation: Evaluation focused on whether, to what extent, how, and why an intervention caused outcomes.
process evaluation: Evaluation of whether and how an intervention is implemented, how it operates, and why delivery varies.
proportionality: Matching evaluation effort, design, cost, and transparency to risk, scale, uncertainty, and decision value.
value for money: Evaluation of costs, benefits, efficiency, economy, effectiveness, equity, and overall public value.
accountability: Use of evaluation evidence to support scrutiny, transparency, spending decisions, and public confidence.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Appendix Structure

Glossary and scope.
Preparing for a pir.
Monitoring data.
Stakeholder engagement.
Evaluation evidence.
Case studies.

title: Guidance for Conducting Regulatory Post Implementation Review
text:
Practical Implications

Plan review evidence while the regulation is being designed, not only when the PIR deadline arrives.
Use monitoring and stakeholder evidence proportionately when causal comparison is infeasible.
State clearly what can and cannot be attributed to regulation using the available evidence.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The qualitative quality guide provides a framework for judging whether qualitative evaluation evidence is defensible, transparent, useful, and fit for purpose.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
This appendix gives a framework for appraising qualitative evaluations, especially those concerned with policy, programmes, and practice. It is built from literature review, government evaluation practice, existing quality frameworks, and expert consultation.
The framework is mainly designed for assessing written outputs, but selected questions can also inform proposals, protocols, training, and research management.
Quality assessment focuses on the findings and on the research process behind them: design, sampling, data collection, analysis, reporting, reflexivity, ethics, neutrality, and auditability.
The guide emphasises professional judgment. Quality indicators support appraisal, but the assessor still has to decide what matters most for the evaluation’s purpose, method, and policy context.
For close reading, track how this note uses qualitative quality, data quality, professional judgment, and auditability while moving through framework purpose, scope, and application. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the guidance asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider Magenta Book route, this appendix supplies specialist depth for the main guidance. It should be read after the relevant chapter has established the evaluation purpose, questions, data constraints, and use needs.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

qualitative quality: Judgment about whether qualitative design, sampling, data, analysis, and reporting are fit for purpose.
data quality: The fitness of evaluation data, including relevance, validity, completeness, timeliness, and bias control.
professional judgment: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
auditability: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
reflexivity: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.
evaluation ethics: Attention to rights, consent, privacy, harm, fairness, and appropriate handling of participants and data.
fitness for purpose: A recurring idea used to frame evaluation design, evidence, interpretation, quality, or use.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Appendix Structure

Framework purpose.
Scope.
Application.
Guiding principles.
Appraisal questions.
Quality indicators.

title: Quality in Qualitative Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Assess qualitative findings together with the design and analysis process that produced them.
Use quality questions to strengthen proposals and protocols, not only completed reports.
Keep judgment proportionate to the evaluation purpose and method rather than applying a mechanical checklist.
