title: Chapter 01: Fundamentals of Program Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation is an applied practice that must balance scientific credibility, stakeholder credibility, program stage, and practical usefulness.

title: Chapter 01: Fundamentals of Program Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 1 introduces Chen’s practical orientation to evaluation. It treats program evaluation as more than method selection: evaluators need to understand intervention programs, stakeholder needs, program politics, and the purposes that evidence is expected to serve.
The chapter extends the familiar formative/summative distinction into a fundamental typology. Evaluation can serve constructive or conclusive functions, and it can focus on process, outcome, or hybrid combinations of the two.
A central tension is the need to address both scientific and stakeholder credibility. Scientific credibility asks whether evidence is technically sound; stakeholder credibility asks whether the evaluation takes users’ concerns, practices, and decisions seriously enough to be useful.
The integrated evaluation perspective sets up the rest of the book. Instead of privileging method-driven rigor or stakeholder responsiveness alone, Chen argues for designs that synthesize both requirements in ways that fit real programs.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses fundamental evaluation typology, constructive evaluation, conclusive evaluation, and hybrid evaluation while moving through intervention programs and feedback, fundamental evaluation typology, and internal and external evaluators. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the foundations sequence: it defines evaluation fit, typology, and program theory before any stage-specific evaluation is selected.

title: Chapter 01: Fundamentals of Program Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

fundamental evaluation typology: Chen’s early matrix distinguishing constructive and conclusive functions across process and outcome stages.
constructive evaluation: Evaluation used to improve, clarify, develop, or strengthen a program before or during implementation.
conclusive evaluation: Evaluation used to judge merit, worth, performance, effectiveness, or accountability.
hybrid evaluation: Evaluation that intentionally combines constructive and conclusive functions, or spans more than one program stage.
scientific credibility: The degree to which an evaluation is judged technically sound, systematic, and governed by defensible inquiry principles.
stakeholder credibility: The degree to which stakeholders judge that an evaluation reflects their views, concerns, needs, and practical realities.
integrated evaluation perspective: Chen’s stance that evaluation should synthesize scientific credibility and stakeholder responsiveness.

title: Chapter 01: Fundamentals of Program Evaluation
text:
Chapter Structure

Intervention programs and feedback.
Fundamental evaluation typology.
Internal and external evaluators.
Stakeholder credibility.
Scientific credibility.
Integrated evaluation perspective.

title: Chapter 01: Fundamentals of Program Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Classify the evaluation function before choosing methods.
Treat stakeholder credibility as a design requirement, not a courtesy.
Use the integrated perspective to avoid rigid method-first evaluation.

title: Chapter 03: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema (Program Theory)
text:
Core Argument
Logic models and the action model/change model schema are complementary tools for describing programs, with the schema adding context, implementation, and causal mechanisms when a simple logic model is too thin.

title: Chapter 03: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema (Program Theory)
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 3 gives evaluators two program-description tools. Logic models are compact and useful for communication, grant requirements, and reducing complexity into manageable components.
Program theory is treated as a richer framework. Chen’s action model/change model schema separates the causal logic of change from the practical arrangements required to deliver the intervention.
The change model focuses on goals, determinants, and interventions. The action model focuses on implementing organizations, implementers, partners, context, target population, and service delivery protocols.
The chapter does not reject logic models. It argues that evaluators should choose the representation that fits the program and evaluation purpose, using the fuller schema when causal mechanisms, context, feedback loops, or implementation assumptions matter.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses logic model, program theory, action model, and change model while moving through logic models, program theory, and change model components. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the foundations sequence: it defines evaluation fit, typology, and program theory before any stage-specific evaluation is selected.

title: Chapter 03: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema (Program Theory)
text:
Key Concepts

logic model: A compact representation of program inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes used for planning and communication.
program theory: The explicit or implicit assumptions linking action, context, mechanisms, and outcomes in an intervention.
action model: The program theory component specifying the organizations, implementers, partners, context, target population, and delivery arrangements needed for action.
change model: The program theory component explaining how intervention activities are expected to change determinants and outcomes.
causal mechanism: The process through which an intervention is expected to affect determinants or outcomes.
contextual factors: Setting conditions, resources, relationships, constraints, and supports that shape implementation or outcomes.
feedback loops: Information paths through which monitoring, process, or outcome evidence can improve program design and implementation.

title: Chapter 03: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema (Program Theory)
text:
Chapter Structure

Logic models.
Program theory.
Change model components.
Action model components.
Relationships among components.
Applications.

title: Chapter 03: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema (Program Theory)
text:
Practical Implications

Use logic models when communication and basic structure are enough.
Use the action model/change model schema when context and mechanisms drive evaluation risk.
Clarify stakeholder theory before designing data collection.

title: Chapter 05: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Action Plan
text:
Core Argument
Action-plan evaluation tests whether the program has the organizational, human, partnership, contextual, and delivery arrangements needed to implement the intervention.

title: Chapter 05: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Action Plan
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 5 completes the planning-stage sequence by focusing on the action model. If Chapter 4 clarifies what change is intended, Chapter 5 asks whether stakeholders have a practical plan for making that change happen.
The action model framework directs attention to implementers, implementing organizations, partners, ecological context, target populations, and delivery protocols. These components are interdependent, so a change to one component can alter the feasibility of the whole plan.
Conceptualization facilitation helps stakeholders develop or improve the action plan. The evaluator’s role is not merely to review documents, but to surface missing components, weak assumptions, and practical implementation risks.
Pilot testing then provides field-based feedback before full implementation. It tests whether proposed activities, dosage, scheduling, settings, staff roles, and client responses are workable at a smaller scale.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses action plan, action model, conceptualization facilitation, and pilot testing while moving through action model framework, conceptualization facilitation, and commentary on action plans. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the planning sequence: it helps stakeholders clarify program scope and action plan before implementation begins.

title: Chapter 05: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Action Plan
text:
Key Concepts

action plan: The planning account of how the intervention will be organized, staffed, delivered, supported, and adapted in practice.
action model: The program theory component specifying the organizations, implementers, partners, context, target population, and delivery arrangements needed for action.
conceptualization facilitation: Evaluator support for helping stakeholders articulate program scope, action plan, and underlying assumptions.
pilot testing: Small-scale trial of intervention or implementation arrangements before full-scale launch.
implementing organization: The organization responsible for arranging resources, staff, partnerships, and routines needed to deliver the intervention.
ecological context: The surrounding social, organizational, community, policy, and interpersonal conditions that support or constrain implementation.
service delivery protocols: Procedures that specify how, where, when, and by whom services are delivered to participants.

title: Chapter 05: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Action Plan
text:

Action model framework.
Conceptualization facilitation.
Commentary on action plans.
Pilot testing principles.
Designing pilot tests.
Revising the plan.

title: Chapter 05: Helping Stakeholders Clarify a Program Plan: Action Plan
text:
Practical Implications

Assess implementer capacity and organizational support before launch.
Use pilot testing to find practical delivery problems early.
Treat the action plan as a theory that can be improved, not as an administrative formality.

title: Chapter 07: Assessing Implementation in the Mature Implementation Stage
text:
Core Argument
Mature implementation can support conclusive process evaluation, but evaluators may still need constructive or hybrid designs when accountability and improvement both matter.

title: Chapter 07: Assessing Implementation in the Mature Implementation Stage
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 7 asks how to evaluate implementation once program procedures are routine. At this stage, stakeholders may need accountability evidence about whether the intervention is being delivered as intended.
Fidelity evaluation is the main conclusive process approach. It can focus on intervention fidelity, referral fidelity, service delivery fidelity, target population fidelity, or other action-model components.
The chapter also addresses the tension between fidelity and reinvention. Adaptation can be valuable during early development, but excessive deviation in mature implementation can dilute program integrity unless a defensible reason for change exists.
Theory-driven process evaluation provides a hybrid alternative. It asks not only whether implementation matches the plan, but how implementation components contribute to the program’s quality and performance.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses mature implementation, conclusive evaluation, fidelity evaluation, and reinvention while moving through constructive process evaluation in maturity, conclusive process evaluation, and fidelity approaches. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the implementation sequence: it separates early troubleshooting, mature implementation assessment, and routine monitoring.

title: Chapter 07: Assessing Implementation in the Mature Implementation Stage
text:
Key Concepts

mature implementation: A program stage in which delivery routines and rules are sufficiently established for fidelity, monitoring, or hybrid process assessment.
conclusive evaluation: Evaluation used to judge merit, worth, performance, effectiveness, or accountability.
fidelity evaluation: Assessment of whether implementation matches the intended intervention, delivery, referral, or target-population design.
reinvention: Local modification or adaptation of an intervention during implementation.
theory-driven process evaluation: Hybrid process evaluation that examines how implementation components contribute to program quality and performance.
hybrid evaluation: Evaluation that intentionally combines constructive and conclusive functions, or spans more than one program stage.
implementation quality: The degree to which actual delivery is adequate, consistent, appropriate, and aligned with the program plan.

title: Chapter 07: Assessing Implementation in the Mature Implementation Stage
text:
Chapter Structure

Constructive process evaluation in maturity.
Conclusive process evaluation.
Fidelity approaches.
Fidelity versus reinvention.
Theory-driven process evaluation.
Design fit.

title: Chapter 07: Assessing Implementation in the Mature Implementation Stage
text:
Practical Implications

Use fidelity evaluation when accountability for implementation quality is central.
Distinguish useful adaptation from damaging drift.
Use theory-driven process evaluation when stakeholders need both judgment and explanation.

title: Chapter 09: Constructive Outcome Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Constructive outcome evaluation improves goals, evaluability, plausibility, and stakeholder consensus before a program is judged by outcome evidence.

title: Chapter 09: Constructive Outcome Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 9 focuses on outcome-stage work that is still developmental. The aim is to strengthen the program’s coherence and readiness for conclusive outcome evaluation.
SMART goals help stakeholders develop goals that are specific and measurable. Evaluability assessment checks whether the program is organized well enough for a rigorous outcome evaluation to be meaningful.
Plausibility assessment/consensus building addresses two common problems: official goals may not reflect what the program is actually doing, and stakeholder groups may not agree on which goals should be evaluated.
Chen also warns against the goal trap. Evaluators should not take official goals at face value when they are symbolic, unrealistic, vague, or disconnected from operative practice.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses constructive outcome evaluation, SMART goals, evaluability assessment, and plausibility assessment while moving through purpose of constructive outcome work, SMART goals, and evaluability assessment. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the outcome sequence: it moves from readiness for outcome evaluation to experimentation, real-world effectuality, and theory-driven explanation.

title: Chapter 09: Constructive Outcome Evaluations
text:
Key Concepts

constructive outcome evaluation: Outcome-stage work that strengthens goals, evaluability, coherence, and stakeholder agreement before conclusive outcome assessment.
SMART goals: Goals specified so they can be meaningfully understood, measured, and used for outcome evaluation.
evaluability assessment: Assessment of whether a program is coherent, measurable, and ready for credible outcome evaluation.
plausibility assessment: Inquiry into which official, operative, intended, and unintended goals are credible enough to guide evaluation.
goal trap: A problem created when official goals are accepted uncritically even though they are vague, implausible, symbolic, or disconnected from program reality.
operative goals: Goals actually pursued through program behavior, whether or not they match official goals.
consensus building: Evaluator-facilitated agreement among stakeholder groups about goals, outcomes, assumptions, or evaluation focus.

title: Chapter 09: Constructive Outcome Evaluations
text:
Chapter Structure

Purpose of constructive outcome work.
Smart goals.
Evaluability assessment.
Goal traps.
Plausibility assessment.
Consensus building.

title: Chapter 09: Constructive Outcome Evaluations
text:
Practical Implications

Clarify and test goals before measuring them.
Use evaluability assessment to avoid premature outcome evaluation.
Surface operative and unintended goals before finalizing evaluation outcomes.

title: Chapter 11: The Holistic Effectuality Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Holistic effectuality evaluation reframes real-world outcome evaluation as a hybrid assessment of program coherence, capacity, joint effects, adjuvants, and bias.

title: Chapter 11: The Holistic Effectuality Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 11 develops an alternative to the experimentation approach. Chen argues that real-world outcome evaluation often needs to assess the joint effects of an intervention and the adjuvants that help it work.
The approach is hybrid. Constructive assessment first checks coherence, capacity, program theory, stakeholder participation, and vulnerabilities. Conclusive assessment then examines real-world effects without automatically treating every support or adaptation as a validity threat.
The chapter is not anti-experimentation. It argues that the experimentation approach and holistic effectuality approach serve different priorities, and evaluators should choose by stakeholder need and program circumstance.
The checklist for ranking real-world evaluations operationalizes this approach by rating constructive and conclusive components together, rather than ranking designs only by conventional experimental hierarchy.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses holistic effectuality evaluation, real-world outcome evaluation, adjuvant, and constructive assessment while moving through limits of experimentation logic, hybrid real-world outcome evaluation, and constructive assessment. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the outcome sequence: it moves from readiness for outcome evaluation to experimentation, real-world effectuality, and theory-driven explanation.

title: Chapter 11: The Holistic Effectuality Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Key Concepts

holistic effectuality evaluation: A real-world outcome approach that combines constructive and conclusive assessment and examines joint effects of intervention and adjuvants.
real-world outcome evaluation: Outcome evaluation focused on how an intervention works under practical delivery conditions, including supports and adaptations.
adjuvant: A real-world support, adaptation, or contextual aid that helps an intervention work but may look like a validity threat in conventional experiments.
constructive assessment: The improvement-oriented component of an evaluation that checks coherence, capacity, vulnerabilities, and readiness.
conclusive assessment: The judgment-oriented component of an evaluation, especially about implementation quality or real-world effects.
joint effects: Effects produced by an intervention together with real-world supports, adaptations, and contextual adjuvants.
checklist: A structured set of criteria used to rate or review evaluation quality, readiness, or completeness.

title: Chapter 11: The Holistic Effectuality Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Chapter Structure

Limits of experimentation logic.
Hybrid real-world outcome evaluation.
Constructive assessment.
Conclusive assessment.
Adjuvants.
Ranking real-world evaluations.

title: Chapter 11: The Holistic Effectuality Evaluation Approach to Outcome Evaluation
text:
Practical Implications

Assess whether real-world supports are part of the intervention rather than mere threats.
Combine readiness work with outcome assessment when programs are not yet coherent.
Use design hierarchy cautiously when stakeholder usefulness depends on real-world effectuality.

title: Chapter 13: What to Do If Your Logic Model Does Not Work as Well as Expected
text:
Core Argument
Logic models often help, but programs that mix intervention delivery with system change may need the action model/change model schema to clarify context, partnership, and causal mechanisms.

title: Chapter 13: What to Do If Your Logic Model Does Not Work as Well as Expected
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 13 revisits logic models through two applied cases. The point is not that logic models are useless, but that they can be too limited for programs where system change is part of the intervention.
In such cases, stakeholders may struggle to distinguish activities, contextual change, intervention components, and outcomes. A standard logic model can compress these distinctions until the model becomes confusing or misleading.
The action model/change model schema helps by making context, partnership, community awareness, needs assessment, interventions, and mechanisms more explicit.
Chen offers reactive and proactive options. Evaluators can continue with logic models and add the schema when problems arise, or use the schema from the outset when they know the program involves system change and contextual mechanisms.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses logic model, system change, action model, and change model while moving through logic-model limitations, diversity enhancement case, and community health initiative case. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the advanced sequence: it tests the limits of common tools and connects evaluation evidence to theory, system change, and dissemination.

title: Chapter 13: What to Do If Your Logic Model Does Not Work as Well as Expected
text:
Key Concepts

logic model: A compact representation of program inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes used for planning and communication.
system change: Intervention work aimed at changing organizational, community, partnership, or contextual arrangements, not only delivering a discrete service.
action model: The program theory component specifying the organizations, implementers, partners, context, target population, and delivery arrangements needed for action.
change model: The program theory component explaining how intervention activities are expected to change determinants and outcomes.
reactive approach: A strategy of continuing with a logic model until limitations appear, then adding the action model/change model schema.
proactive approach: A strategy of using the action model/change model schema early when logic-model limitations are predictable.
contextual factors: Setting conditions, resources, relationships, constraints, and supports that shape implementation or outcomes.

text:

text:
Practical Implications

Check whether a logic model is hiding system-change work.
Use the action model/change model schema when context and partnership are central.
Choose reactive or proactive supplementation based on foreseeable model risk.

title: Chapter 15: Evaluation and Dissemination: Top-down Approach versus Bottom-up Approach
text:
Core Argument
Dissemination should not rely only on top-down efficacy evidence; real-world evaluation also needs viable, effectual, and transferable cogency.

title: Chapter 15: Evaluation and Dissemination: Top-down Approach versus Bottom-up Approach
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 15 closes the book by connecting evaluation to dissemination and evidence-based intervention debates.
The top-down approach privileges efficacy evidence, often from randomized controlled trials, before intervention spread. Chen argues that this can be too narrow for social betterment and health promotion programs, where stakeholder practice, feasibility, and context matter.
The integrative cogency model reframes credible evidence as multidimensional. Evaluators need evidence about viability, effectuality, and transferability rather than only a single internal-validity-centered claim.
The bottom-up approach reverses the usual order: begin with real-world viability and effectiveness, improve the intervention through practical feedback, and then use more controlled efficacy evidence when it adds value.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses top-down approach, bottom-up approach, integrative cogency model, and viable cogency while moving through top-down dissemination, limits of evidence-based intervention, and integrative cogency model. The notes below identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what design problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the advanced sequence: it tests the limits of common tools and connects evaluation evidence to theory, system change, and dissemination.

title: Chapter 15: Evaluation and Dissemination: Top-down Approach versus Bottom-up Approach
text:
Key Concepts

top-down approach: A dissemination route that privileges efficacy evidence, often from controlled trials, before real-world spread.
bottom-up approach: A dissemination route that begins with real-world viability and effectiveness before moving toward more controlled efficacy evidence.
integrative cogency model: A model that treats credible evidence as multidimensional: viable, effectual, and transferable.
viable cogency: Evidence about whether an intervention can work as a feasible real-world program.
effectual cogency: Evidence about whether an intervention affects intended outcomes.
transferable cogency: Evidence about whether viability and effectuality can transfer across real-world settings.
evidence-based intervention: An intervention promoted for wider use because evidence supports claims about its effectiveness or suitability.

title: Chapter 15: Evaluation and Dissemination: Top-down Approach versus Bottom-up Approach
text:
Chapter Structure

Top-down dissemination.
Limits of evidence-based intervention.
Integrative cogency model.
Bottom-up approach.
Viability and effectiveness.
Method contingency.

title: Chapter 15: Evaluation and Dissemination: Top-down Approach versus Bottom-up Approach
text:
Practical Implications

Assess real-world viability before assuming an intervention is ready to disseminate.
Use evidence standards that match social-program complexity.
Treat RCT evidence as powerful for some questions, not as the only credible evidence.

title: Core Concepts in Practical Program Evaluation
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Practical Program Evaluation (Chen 2015).

title: Core Concepts in Practical Program Evaluation
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Practical Program Evaluation (Chen 2015).

title: Core Concepts in Practical Program Evaluation
text:
Central Logic
The book treats practical evaluation as a staged, contingent design problem: clarify the program, identify its stage, select constructive, conclusive, or hybrid approaches, and judge evidence by usefulness as well as technical credibility.

title: Core Concepts in Practical Program Evaluation
text:
Concepts

Evaluation Fit

fundamental evaluation typology: Chen’s early matrix distinguishing constructive and conclusive functions across process and outcome stages.
comprehensive evaluation typology: Chen’s map of evaluation approaches by program stage, evaluation function, and strategy.
constructive evaluation: Evaluation used to improve, clarify, develop, or strengthen a program before or during implementation.
conclusive evaluation: Evaluation used to judge merit, worth, performance, effectiveness, or accountability.
hybrid evaluation: Evaluation that intentionally combines constructive and conclusive functions, or spans more than one program stage.



Program Theory

logic model: A compact representation of program inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes used for planning and communication.
program theory: The explicit or implicit assumptions linking action, context, mechanisms, and outcomes in an intervention.
action model: The program theory component specifying the organizations, implementers, partners, context, target population, and delivery arrangements needed for action.
change model: The program theory component explaining how intervention activities are expected to change determinants and outcomes.
stakeholder theory: Program theory based primarily on stakeholders’ experience, assumptions, and local knowledge.
formal theory: A theory from research literature or a scientific tradition used to design or explain an intervention.



Planning and Implementation

program scope: The planning account of problem, goals, target population, intervention, determinants, and expected outcomes.
action plan: The planning account of how the intervention will be organized, staffed, delivered, supported, and adapted in practice.
needs assessment: Planning-stage inquiry into unmet needs, service gaps, target groups, and priorities.
pilot testing: Small-scale trial of intervention or implementation arrangements before full-scale launch.
formative evaluation: Development-oriented evaluation during implementation that identifies barriers, facilitators, and remedies.
fidelity evaluation: Assessment of whether implementation matches the intended intervention, delivery, referral, or target-population design.



Monitoring and Outcomes

program monitoring: Periodic quantitative tracking of program process and outcomes to support management and accountability.
constructive outcome evaluation: Outcome-stage work that strengthens goals, evaluability, coherence, and stakeholder agreement before conclusive outcome assessment.
validity-focused outcome evaluation: Outcome evaluation guided by experimentation logic and the priority of ruling out threats to internal validity.
holistic effectuality evaluation: A real-world outcome approach that combines constructive and conclusive assessment and examines joint effects of intervention and adjuvants.
theory-driven outcome evaluation: Outcome evaluation that examines causal mechanisms and implementation processes, not only whether outcomes changed.



Evidence and Dissemination

adjuvant: A real-world support, adaptation, or contextual aid that helps an intervention work but may look like a validity threat in conventional experiments.
system change: Intervention work aimed at changing organizational, community, partnership, or contextual arrangements, not only delivering a discrete service.
integrative cogency model: A model that treats credible evidence as multidimensional: viable, effectual, and transferable.
viable cogency: Evidence about whether an intervention can work as a feasible real-world program.
effectual cogency: Evidence about whether an intervention affects intended outcomes.
transferable cogency: Evidence about whether viability and effectuality can transfer across real-world settings.

title: Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the chapter notes from Practical Program Evaluation (Chen 2015).

title: Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on the chapter notes from Practical Program Evaluation (Chen 2015).

title: Practical Program Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Begin by identifying the program stage and the evaluation function: constructive, conclusive, or hybrid.
Use the comprehensive typology as a routing tool, not as a rigid checklist.
Clarify program theory before choosing measures or causal designs.
Separate program scope from action plan so change logic and implementation arrangements can both be tested.
Use formative and troubleshooting approaches during initial implementation rather than demanding premature accountability evidence.
Use fidelity or theory-driven process evaluation once implementation is mature enough to judge.
Treat monitoring as useful for performance tracking, but insufficient for causal claims.
Use constructive outcome evaluation before conclusive outcome evaluation when goals, evaluability, or stakeholder consensus are weak.
Choose experimentation, holistic effectuality, or theory-driven outcome evaluation according to the question stakeholders actually need answered.
Use logic models carefully when programs involve system change, contextual mechanisms, or partnership work.
Assess dissemination evidence through viability, effectuality, and transferability rather than efficacy alone.

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
2015MCP evaluation-textsStatus: needs_reviewSource pages 79Major section

This note summarises the MCP research-library section “Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema” from Huey T. Chen’s Practical Program Evaluation (Chen 2015). It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked retrieval records, not a substitute for the source text.

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
Core Argument
The chapter positions logic models and the action model/change model schema as complementary tools for describing interventions.

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
Study Summary
The chapter positions logic models and the action model/change model schema as complementary tools for describing interventions.
Logic models help simplify a complicated program into components, while the schema gives evaluators a fuller way to address contextual factors and causal mechanisms.
The distinction matters because many evaluations fail when the representation is too simple for the program’s actual operating logic.

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
Key Concepts

Logic model
Action model
Change model
Contextual factors
Causal mechanisms

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema

title: Logic Models and the Action Model/Change Model Schema
text:
Practical Implications

Use logic models for communication and initial structure.
Use the action model/change model schema when implementation arrangements and causal assumptions require more detail.
Choose the representation that supports evaluation decisions rather than the one stakeholders already know best.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 30-41 Chapter 1

This note summarises Chapter 1, “The Essence of Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Program theory turns evaluation from a simple before-and-after judgement into an explanation of how an intervention is expected to generate change, where that explanation might fail, and what evidence would distinguish success, partial success, implementation failure, and theory failure.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The opening chapter uses a deliberately simple health example to show why an evaluation without program theory can identify an outcome pattern but cannot say much about why it occurred. A black-box evaluation can report whether results changed, but it gives weak guidance about which mechanisms worked, which activities mattered, or what should be changed next.
The chapter then reframes evaluation around a model of how resources, activities, outputs, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, assumptions, and contextual factors are connected. This makes the evaluation more diagnostic: results can be interpreted against the expected chain of contribution rather than treated as a single program-versus-outcome comparison.
A central practical point is that failure, partial success, and apparent success all need explanation. Program theory helps evaluators identify whether the intervention was implemented as intended, whether the causal logic was plausible, whether context disrupted the pathway, and whether observed results can be credibly attributed to the program.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Black-box evaluation: an evaluation that relates an intervention to outcomes without unpacking the intervening causal pathway.
Program theory: an explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to intended outcomes.
Implementation failure versus theory failure: a poor result may reflect weak delivery, weak causal assumptions, or both.
Partial success: evidence may support some links in the chain while exposing weak or missing links elsewhere.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Start evaluation design by asking what must be true for the intervention to work.
Collect evidence along the causal chain, not only at the final outcome.
Use the theory to diagnose why results occurred and what should be improved.

text:

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 64-81 Chapter 3

This note summarises Chapter 3, “Common Myths and Traps,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Core Argument
The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Study Summary
The chapter clears away myths that weaken practice: program theory is not brand new, there is not one correct way to do it, it can be a serious evaluation model, and it does not necessarily require excessive time. The authors also challenge the idea that a theory is untestable or that causality can simply be assumed once a model is drawn.
The traps are more operational. Program theory can become a compliance artifact, a vague outcomes list, an overcomplicated wall chart, or a model that ignores unintended outcomes and contextual variation. A particularly important trap is developing a theory for an evaluation and then failing to use it in evaluation design, data collection, analysis, and reporting.
The chapter’s stance is pragmatic: program theory should be purposeful, proportionate, explicit, and used. A modest but well-targeted theory is better than an elaborate representation that nobody uses to make decisions.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Key Concepts

Myth: a mistaken belief about what program theory is or requires.
Trap: a recurring practice problem that undermines the usefulness of a program theory.
Purposeful proportionality: investing enough effort for the intended use without turning the exercise into an end in itself.
Unintended results: outcomes that are outside the intended chain but still matter for evaluation.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Practical Implications

Choose the level of detail to match the decision or evaluation use.
Build in explicit attention to unintended outcomes and alternative explanations.
Make sure the final theory controls actual choices about measures, methods, and reporting.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Part 2: Assessing Your Circumstances Source PDF pages 96-121 Chapter 5

This note summarises Chapter 5, “The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Core Argument
The nature of the intervention and its setting should shape the program theory; simple, complicated, and complex situations require different assumptions about focus, governance, consistency, necessity, sufficiency, and change trajectories.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Study Summary
The chapter introduces distinctions among simple, complicated, and complex interventions. Simple situations have relatively direct and stable pathways. Complicated situations involve multiple components, actors, sites, or technical dependencies. Complex situations involve adaptation, emergence, feedback, and context-sensitive pathways.
The authors use these distinctions to warn against forcing every intervention into a linear model. A program theory may need to show multiple strands, variable implementation, different causal mechanisms in different settings, or pathways that emerge through learning. Governance and consistency also matter because centrally controlled programs differ from networked or locally adapted interventions.
The chapter’s practical value is diagnostic. Before choosing a representation or evaluation design, evaluators should ask what kind of intervention they are dealing with and what type of causal claim is reasonable.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Key Concepts

Simple, complicated, complex: a continuum for thinking about intervention structure and causal predictability.
Necessariness and sufficiency: whether a component is required for success and whether it can generate success on its own.
Change trajectory: the expected pattern and timing of change.
Governance and consistency: how control, variation, and adaptation affect the theory.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Practical Implications

Do not use a single linear chain when the intervention has interacting strands or adaptive pathways.
Use context and governance analysis to decide how general the theory can be.
Treat complexity as a design issue for monitoring and evaluation, not an excuse for vagueness.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 176-225 Chapter 7

This note summarises Chapter 7, “Developing a Theory of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Core Argument
A theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem analysis to intended outcomes; it requires situation analysis, evidence-informed causal reasoning, careful focusing, and a defensible outcomes chain.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Study Summary
The chapter focuses on the change side of program theory: what problem is being addressed, what causes or contributing factors sustain it, what opportunities can be used, and what sequence of outcomes would indicate meaningful change. Situation analysis is presented as the foundation for a credible theory of change.
The authors stress that causes, consequences, and opportunities should be investigated rather than assumed. Research evidence can inform which causal factors are plausible and which strategies are likely to influence them. Focusing and scoping are then needed because no program can address every cause, consequence, population, and context at once.
The outcomes chain is the central device in the chapter. It connects early, intermediate, and long-term outcomes in a way that shows consequential progression rather than just listing desired results. A good outcomes chain makes the causal story inspectable and testable.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Key Concepts

Situation analysis: structured understanding of the problem, causes, consequences, and opportunities.
Outcomes chain: a sequence of linked outcomes showing expected progression toward longer-term change.
Focusing and scoping: deliberate choices about what the program will and will not address.
Causal plausibility: the extent to which the proposed pathway is supported by reasoning and evidence.

title: 7. Developing a Theory of Change
text:
Practical Implications

Do not start with activities; start with the problem and causal conditions.
Use research and local evidence to identify leverage points.
Build outcomes chains that distinguish immediate outputs from meaningful changes in people, systems, or conditions.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:

This note summarises Chapter 9, “Representing Program Theory,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Representing program theory is a design choice: diagrams, matrices, narratives, and multiple models should be selected to fit the intervention’s causal structure, complexity, and intended use.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This chapter surveys representation options, including pipeline logic models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and multiple linked models. It treats representation as more than presentation: the form chosen shapes what users notice, question, and can use.
The authors distinguish representations for complicated and complex programs. Multiple organizations, sequential interventions, parallel causal strands, context-specific mechanisms, emergent theory of action, vertical integration, and systems concepts may all require alternatives to a single diagram.
The chapter ends with practical quality criteria. Good representations explain contribution, avoid dead ends, make arrows meaningful, show direction of change, focus on key elements, avoid clutter, and remove material that does not add meaning. The test is whether the representation clarifies the theory for its intended use.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Pipeline logic model: a representation moving from inputs and activities through outputs and outcomes.
Outcomes chain model: a representation centered on consequential outcome progression.
Realist matrix: a representation that links context, mechanism, and outcome patterns.
Representational economy: including enough to guide use without overwhelming interpretation.

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Chapter Structure

Representing Complex Program Theory

title: 9. Representing Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Choose representation after clarifying use and intervention complexity.
Use multiple models when one view cannot serve all users or levels.
Review diagrams for meaningful arrows, missing assumptions, and avoidable clutter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Part 4: Resources for Developing Program Theory Source PDF pages 346-377 Chapter 11

This note summarises Chapter 11, “Some Research-Based Theories of Change,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Core Argument
Research-based theories of change can strengthen program theory when they are selected critically and adapted to the intervention context rather than imported mechanically.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Study Summary
This chapter introduces several families of research-based theory, including reasoned action and planned behavior, stages of change, empowerment, diffusion, socioecological theory, and network theory. Each offers concepts that can help explain how change might occur.
The authors do not present these theories as universal templates. Each has limits, assumptions, and domains of usefulness. The task is to use research-based theory to sharpen causal reasoning, identify intermediate outcomes, and choose measures, while still checking whether the theory fits the specific program and context.
The chapter is especially useful as a bridge between general social science and practical evaluation. It shows how evaluators can use established theory to avoid purely local speculation without losing attention to implementation realities.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Key Concepts

Research-based theory: formal or empirical theory that can inform causal assumptions in a program theory.
Diffusion: the spread of ideas or practices through populations, networks, or systems.
Socioecological theory: attention to multiple levels of influence, from individual to institutional and environmental.
Theory selection: choosing concepts because they explain the intervention, not because they are familiar.

title: 11. Some Research-Based Theories of Change
text:
Practical Implications

Use formal theory to identify mechanisms, assumptions, and intermediate outcomes.
State the limitations of imported theory and adapt it to context.
Avoid mixing theories without explaining how their mechanisms relate.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources

This note summarises Chapter 13, “Logic Models Resources,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Core Argument
Logic model resources and technologies can support representation, but the usefulness of a logic model depends more on conceptual clarity than on the drawing tool.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Study Summary
This chapter reviews pipeline logic models, variations of outcomes chain models, drawn models, standard software, specialist visual tools, and dedicated logic model packages. It gives evaluators a practical sense of available representation formats.
The chapter reinforces earlier cautions: a visually tidy model can still be conceptually weak, and a complex tool can obscure rather than clarify the theory. Technology should support the intended use, audience, and complexity of the program theory.
The practical emphasis is on choosing the right representation environment. Some situations need quick sketches to support dialogue; others need formal models for documentation, monitoring, or reporting. The tool should make revision, communication, and use easier.

title: 13. Logic Models Resources
text:
Key Concepts

Pipeline model variation: different ways to organize inputs, activities, outputs, and outcomes.
Outcomes-chain variation: different degrees of detail, nesting, and temporal structure in outcome pathways.
Representation technology: software or visual methods used to create and maintain logic models.
Tool fit: alignment between representation medium, audience, and purpose.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Part 5: Using Program Theory for Monitoring and Evaluation Source PDF pages 496-527 Chapter 15

This note summarises Chapter 15, “Causal Inference,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Core Argument
Causal inference in program theory should be both scientific and pragmatic, combining congruence analysis, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review to assess whether the program plausibly contributed to observed outcomes.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Study Summary
The chapter argues against polarized thinking about causal inference. Strong causal analysis requires rigor, but rigor does not always mean one design. Program theory helps evaluators choose and combine methods that are appropriate to the intervention, question, and available evidence.
The authors organize causal analysis around congruence, counterfactual comparisons, and critical review. Congruence examines whether observed patterns match the theory across intermediate and final outcomes. Counterfactual comparisons ask what would have happened without the program. Critical review tests alternative explanations, exceptions, and weak links.
The chapter is especially important for complicated and complex interventions, where single average effects may hide variation. Program theory supports disaggregation, mechanism testing, and careful explanation of why outcomes occurred for whom and under what conditions.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Key Concepts

Congruence analysis: checking whether evidence across the causal chain fits the program theory.
Counterfactual comparison: estimating what would have happened in the absence of the intervention.
Critical review: actively examining alternative explanations and exceptions.
Scientific pragmatism: using credible methods that fit the real evaluation problem.

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Chapter Structure

title: 15. Causal Inference
text:
Practical Implications

Do not treat program theory as a substitute for causal evidence; use it to structure causal tests.
Combine pattern evidence, comparison evidence, and alternative-explanation review.
Disaggregate results where the theory predicts different pathways or effects.

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This synthesis draws on the full set of chapter notes for Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This synthesis draws on the full set of chapter notes for Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Practical Habits

Start by naming the intended users, uses, and timing of the program theory.
Separate the problem analysis and desired change pathway from the program’s activities and delivery model.
Make assumptions explicit enough that they can be tested or monitored.
Choose representation formats for use, not for visual polish.
Critique the theory before using it to select indicators or evaluation methods.
Use the theory to distinguish implementation failure, theory failure, contextual disruption, and partial success.
Report evidence along the causal pathway so conclusions are traceable.

title: Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Design Checklist

What decision or learning need should the program theory support?
Who needs to participate in developing, challenging, or using it?
What problem, causes, contributing factors, and opportunities does the theory identify?
Which outcomes form a genuine causal chain rather than a list of aspirations?
Which activities and outputs are expected to influence the causal factors?
What contextual or external factors could enable or block outcomes?
Which links in the theory are most uncertain, consequential, or worth testing?
What comparison, congruence evidence, or critical review is needed for causal inference?

title: Strategic Qualitative Principles in Practice
text:
Core Argument
The section frames qualitative inquiry as a set of strategic principles that must be adapted to practical field conditions.

title: Strategic Qualitative Principles in Practice
text:
Study Summary
The section frames qualitative inquiry as a set of strategic principles that must be adapted to practical field conditions.
It rejects the idea that qualitative methods only belong in ideal situations; their value often appears when evaluation questions require context-sensitive judgment.
The evaluator’s task is to maintain qualitative discipline while adjusting to real constraints in access, time, and use.

Practical constraints

title: Strategic Qualitative Principles in Practice
text:
Practical Implications

Define what qualitative evidence is expected to clarify before collecting data.
Plan for adaptation while protecting documentation and analytic transparency.
Use qualitative design choices to make trade-offs explicit.

title: Program Models, Theories of Change, and Qualitative Methods
text:
Core Argument
The section shows how qualitative inquiry can test and refine program models by connecting processes, implementation, and outcomes.

title: Program Models, Theories of Change, and Qualitative Methods
text:
Study Summary
The section shows how qualitative inquiry can test and refine program models by connecting processes, implementation, and outcomes.
Qualitative evidence is useful where the formal theory of change is incomplete, contested, or too abstract to explain practice.
This makes qualitative methods a complement to logic models: they can reveal how the model is experienced and enacted.

title: Program Models, Theories of Change, and Qualitative Methods
text:
Practical Implications

Use interviews and observations to test whether the stated program model matches implementation.
Look for mechanisms and unanticipated outcomes that the initial theory missed.
Treat qualitative findings as evidence for revising the theory of change.

title: Core Concepts in Realistic Evaluation
text:
Central Logic
Realist evaluation studies programs through context-mechanism-outcome configurations rather than asking only whether a whole program worked.

title: Core Concepts in Realistic Evaluation
text:

title: Realistic Evaluation Practice Implications
text: This practice synthesis draws on selected notes from Realistic Evaluation (Pawson and Tilley 1997).

title: Realistic Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Specify conjectured CMO configurations before selecting data collection methods.
Analyze variation across contexts and participants instead of averaging it away too quickly.
Use evaluation findings to refine program theory for future adaptation.
Report findings as conditional explanations, not universal effects.

title: Realistic Evaluation Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as prompts during scoping, design review, method selection, and reporting. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s decision context, available evidence, and user needs.

title: Scientific Realism and CMO Hypotheses
text:
Core Argument
This section turns realist philosophy into an evaluation task: identify, articulate, test, and refine CMO configurations.

title: Scientific Realism and CMO Hypotheses
text:
Study Summary
This section turns realist philosophy into an evaluation task: identify, articulate, test, and refine CMO configurations.
The emphasis is on hypotheses about how mechanisms operate under social and cultural conditions.
Realist evidence therefore depends on comparing where the same mechanism appears to work differently.

title: Scientific Realism and CMO Hypotheses
text:

Scientific realism
Hypothesis testing
Mechanism question
Context question

title: Scientific Realism and CMO Hypotheses
text:
Practical Implications

Translate broad program theory into testable CMO hypotheses.
Design comparisons that can expose how context changes mechanism activation.
Use contrary cases as theory-refining evidence.
